exam_notes: Report of boat striking a whale
additional_remarks: On 9/16/15 there was a report that a boat struck a whale in the Oakland Estuary between the outbound bouys 3 and 5. There was blood seen in the water and the whale dove after being struck. The boat prop was bent in the strike. On 9/19/15 the whale was resighted but deceased floating 1-2 miles south of the San Mateo Bridge in the shipping channel near marker 12. On the 9/23 the whale washed ashore at Coyote Hills Regional Park. On 9/24/15 a necropsy was performed at shoreline of Coyote Hills Regional Park. There were no reports of a whale inside the bay during this time frame and due to the lacerations on the dorsal ridge this is the same whale that was struck.

locality_detail: South Salmon Creek Beach, approximately 200 yards south of Bean Avenue entrance.
additional_remarks: Probable Orca Attack. On 18 May 2016, a report came out in the Press Democrat online with a video of a pod of 4-6 Orca attacking a Gray Whale calf with its mother on 14 May 2016 along the Sonoma Coast off of Carmet beach. Based on video stills and photos from this carcass on the beach, as well as the many rake marks on the carcass, it was most likely the same animal in both the orca attack on 14 May 2016 and the carcass on the beach on 16 May 2016.

locality_detail: San Francisco Bay; under the Golden Gate Bridge
additional_remarks: "USCG self-report; While transiting outbound under the Golden Gate Bridge, USCGC TERN reported hull bumping a humpback whale. TERN?s bridge team consisting of the Commanding Officer, Executive Petty Officer, Operations Petty Officer, Engineer Officer, and Conning Officer observed a humpback whale surface 5-10 feet directly off of the bow. Immediately came full astern on both main diesel engines. Bridge team lost sight of the whale, however, no vibration or bump was felt from the bridge. Due to whale?s close proximity to the cutter and as a precautionary measure, General Emergency was set throughout the ship and all hands investigated spaces for damage. No damage to the cutter. whale in question resurfaced seconds later, 50-200 yards east of the Golden Gate Bridge. The whale appeared to be uninjured by the bridge team as TERN remained on scene for 40 minutes following suspected contact for observation. The whale was observed playfully slapping its fin against the surface of the water. Whale departed the area with no visible injuries."
affiliation: NMFS West Coast Region - Long Beach

locality_detail: Bean Hollow State Beach
additional_remarks: Reported on 7/6/16 by a member of the public, floating 200 yards off the beach between Pebble Beach and Bean Hollow, Pescadero, San Mateo Co., California. Animal reported on beach on the evening of 7/7/16 in a cove north of the main Bean Hollow Beach entrance. CAS made an initial response on 7/7/16. Due to the timing of the tides, CAS was only able to take initial data and samples during this brief response. A complete necropsy team from both TMMC and CAS responded on 7/9/16 at 0730h. Based on photos of the flukes, Cascadia Research Collective staff were unable to locate this individual in their identification catalog. See necropsy report for additional information.

locality_detail: Oakland Harbor
exam_notes: Reported falling off the bow of a ship in the harbor
additional_remarks: Identified as fin whale (Balaenoptera physalus) by asymmetrical coloration on mandibles (gray right mandible) and throat grooves, bicolored baleen, overall coloration, and genetics from SWFSC. Animal was originally reported by the United States Coast Guard at Yerba Buena Island 1/5/17 when a vessel, NYK Triton, had what appeared to be a whale size animal or possible whale carcass on its bow. As the vessel turned into Oakland Bar channel, the carcass fell off the bow and sank. The whale was then reported on 1/7/17 floating in Oakland harbor off of Howard Terminal. Two personnel from TMMC went out to assess the animal, take pictures and collect basic samples. Due to the storm with large swells, high winds and rainfall the animal was left floating next to the Howard Terminal. The USCG received a report of the animal floating in the Oakland harbor channel on 1/9/17 causing hazards to passing vessels. The animal was then towed to Angel Island by the Army Corps of Engineers where it was tied off to a pier near Blunt Point until weather conditions improved. Due to repeated intense weather and storm debris clean-up, the Army Corp of Engineers was only able to land the carcass on Angel Island on 1/25/2017. Due to continued bad weather and boat availability, a small team was only able to get to the carcass for a partial necropsy on 2/6/2017. A second team returned to Angel Island to conduct a more complete necropsy on 3/2/2017. See necropsy report for more details.

The whale was identified as an immature animal based on visual examination of the testes.

This entangled whale had been reported in early April in southern California. At the time of entering this Level A data, it was not conclusive whether or not this was the entangled whale.

locality_detail: Agate Beach, approximately 0.25 miles north of the parking lot.
additional_remarks: The carcass was originally reported as floating off of Agate Beach in Marin County the evening of 25 May 2017. The carcass was confirmed on land at Agate Beach (37.899783¿N, 122.712575¿W) the morning of 26 May 2017. Due to the timing of the tides and logistics, three researchers from TMMC and CAS examined the carcass on 5/26/2017, taking initial measurements and samples. A full team conducted a necropsy on 27 May 2017. The carcass was identified as a known animal (CRC-1540) by Cascadia Research Collective on 5/26/2017. CRC-1540 was first identified off California in 1999 and seen in at least 11 different years off California mostly in the Santa Barbara Channel area. Genetic confirmation on the match can be made from a biopsy taken in 2006. She was documented with a calf in two years. There may be additional sighting information from the Sea of Cortez, Mexico since she also matches the CICMAR catalog.

locality_detail: Under Golden Gate Bridge
additional_remarks: Ryan Clapper (Reporting Party) I was in San Francisco last week with my wife and we decided to hike the Golden Gate Bridge. About half way up we realized there were 3 to 4 humpback whales feeding in the bay below us. A boater was cruising by at high speed and never slowed down. He may have seen one of the whales breathing far away and tried to get closer for a photo and went right over a humpback. SFPD works on the bridge and I made them aware of the situation. We couldn¿t get a vessel number from the boat since it was so far away. So I emailed a photo to the SFPD and a local news station that aired it the next day. http://www.gulfster.com/Daily/2017PictureGallery/hump-back-whale-golden-gate/index.html

locality_detail: Point Reyes National Seashore, North Beach, approximately 2 miles north of the parking lot.
other_findings: For additional information see necropsy report.
additional_remarks: The carcass was originally reported washing onto North Beach in Point Reyes National Seashore in Marin County late afternoon 24 December 2017. Due to the timing of the tides, the holiday, and logistics, two researchers from CAS conducted an examination of the carcass on 25 December 2017, taking initial measurements, photos, and samples. A full team from TMMC conducted a necropsy on 26 December 2017. For additional information see necropsy report. Samples collected on 12/25/2017 included: blubber, feces, muscle, skin, cyamids, and right pelvic bone (for CAS archive). Dorsal fin and fluke photos were sent to Cascadia Research Collective and Happywhale.com. This individual was not known to either organization.

locality_detail: Racoon Straits
additional_remarks: Emaciated. Incomplete examination of the cranium and axial skeleton. The carcass was originally reported floating in the shipping channel of San Francisco Bay on March 7th and towed by the Army Corps of Engineers to Angel Island where it was tied off on a west side beach. TMMC and CAS planned to necropsy the whale at low tide (10.30AM) the following morning. A whale of similar size had been reported swimming in the north Bay for over a week, but no information or photos available to determine if this is the same animal.

additional_remarks: Observed floating in Richardson Bay March 28 2018 early morning, towed by Army Corps of Engineers to Angel Island, with rope around remaining section of peduncle with some difficulty. Skin sloughing, bloated. Flukes and distal penduncle missing. Within a tag of gray necrotic tissue is a wire, 6 inches long, white, copper core, deeply entwined with the necrotic tissue

locality_detail: Oakland Harbor near the Jack London Aquatic Center/Estuary Park
additional_remarks: On 5/16/2018 at 0530h NOAA/NMFS (Justin Viezbicke) received a report from SF Bay Harbor Pilots regarding a deceased whale (species unknown- possibly Humpback) draped across the bow of a large ship entering the SF Bay. The whale was observed falling off the bow of ship in turning channel near Oakland. On 5/18/2018 at 0650h, TMMC received a report from a local resident in Oakland regarding a deceased whale partially submerged, wedged between posts and pinned underneath a bridge. On 5/18, the Port of Oakland also reported the whale to CAS and described the location as ¿in the Oakland Harbor near the Jack London Aquatic Center/Estuary Park¿ at 37.790801°N, 122.263630°W. Later on 5/18/2018, the Army Corp of Engineers towed the whale to Angel Island for a necropsy. Due to logistics and winds, the necropsy was conducted on 5/21/2018 by a team from TMMC, CAS, UC Davis, and California Whale Rescue. A crew from BBC/PBS filmed the necropsy for an upcoming documentary.

locality_detail: Floating 2 miles east of the Farallon Islands
additional_remarks: This whale was initially reported on Saturday June 16th by a whale watching vessel at the Farallon Islands. The carcass was floating on her back with an extremely bloated tongue. The carcass ended up on shore at the southern end of Limantour beach on Monday June 18th near Santa Maria Creek. It was identified as the same whale by a notch in the left pectoral flipper. Necropsy was completed by TMMC, CAS, NOAA, and PRNS on 19 June 2018.

additional_remarks: Cause of death is unknown, pending results The carcass of this adult male pygmy sperm whale was in fair body condition with evidence of recent feeding. There was no apparent trauma. The pathological changes were mostly chronic and insignificant. The heart requires histopathology to assess for cardiomyopathy.

locality_detail: On Fort Funston beach, due west of the sand ladder
additional_remarks: partial carcass rolling in surf. tail flukes missing, heavy shark scavenging. only an estimated length was recorded on 10/01/18 a complete piece of baleen washed up on Ocean Beach at Vicente, which was archived with CAS. A flipper also washed up on Ocean Beach near Noriega but was not collected. January 2019 - part of the skull washed up on Ocean Beach, but was not collected

locality_detail: on Beach north of Slide Ranch
additional_remarks: This animal was reported floating offshore late Sunday night 10/7/18. The carcass washed ashore on 10/8/18 just north of Slide Ranch near Muir Beach. Due to the tides the necropsy team was not able access the whale until Thursday morning 10/11/18 and by then the carcass was rolling in the surf. Some estimated lengths were taken, as well as skin and skull fragments but a full necropsy could not be performed. There were skull fragments found on the beach where the whale had been stranded the evening before.

locality_detail: Kehoe Beach South End, Point Reyes National Seashore, Gulf of the Farallones National Marine Sanctuary
additional_remarks: Whale found on the beach dead, but in good condition.

locality_detail: PRNS - On Drake's Beach, west of the parking lot
additional_remarks: tissue sample collected. Carcass was very degraded with no skin available. 05/23/2024 - tissue sample stored in the walk-in freezer at CAS.

locality_detail: Angel Island - Quarry Beach, Angel Island State Park, San Francisco Bay
additional_remarks: Due to advanced decomposition of the tissues and organs, no other necropsy findings other than malnutrition could be determined. Most external parts, like the skin and blubber were reasonably preserved, but internal organs barely recognizable. No bone fractures and no obvious entanglement were present at the time of necropsy. Several skin defects including underneath right flipper and around fluke. Liver in severe decomposition.

locality_detail: San Francisco Bay, under the Alpha tower of the Bay Bridge.
additional_remarks: Due to location of the animal in the water, only a partial necropsy of the animal could be performed. Cause of death unknown. Scant amount of perirenal fat and subcutaneous fat.

locality_detail: California: Contra Costa Co.; Rodeo Marina, floating offshore near the Philips 66 Refinery.
additional_remarks: This animal was initially reported on 4/2 to be floating off shore near the Philip 66 refinery near Rodeo CA and drifting towards the Carquinez Bridge. There were no towing services available at that time so the carcass was left to floating. On 4/3 the carcass was markedly bloated and floating at the mouth of a marina near Rodeo Creek. A team was assembled in case of a potential necropsy. However, once the team got there the whale was still floating and even with a lower tide, the carcass was inaccessible due to mud. The team tied off the whale that evening. On 4/4 Parker Diving service and a team from TMMC towed the whale to Angel Island. At this point in time the animal was still in relatively fresh condition. Due to the timing of tides, weather, and daylight hours the necropsy needed to be postponed until Tuesday April 9th. On 4/9 the animal was high and dry on Angel Island but was now in a moderate state of decomposition. A full necropsy was conducted.

locality_detail: Floating in the San Francisco Bay near the east end of the San Mateo Bridge
additional_remarks: CAS field volunteer, Kate High collected data and the pelvic bone for CAS. See necropsy report for more details.

locality_detail: Phillips 66 Refinery, T-dock at Davis point
additional_remarks: This carcass was first reported on April 13, 2019 lodged under the dock at the Phillips 66 Refinery. After several failed attempts, the carcass was dislodged from under the dock on April 17 and towed to Angel Island for necropsy. The necropsy was completed on April 23, 2019. See necropsy report for more details.

locality_detail: Floating in San Francisco Bay, north of the Richmond bridge near Point Molate.
additional_remarks: This carcass was first reported floating off of Point Molate the morning of April 13, 2019. On April 13th at 5:37pm, it was reported on Red Rock Island, south of the Richmond Bridge. On April 14, it was reported by some boaters as floating in SF Bay at 37.87265N, 122.39953W. It was not seen for two days and was then reported on land on Brooks Island on April 16. The carcass was amputated posterior to the genital slit and was missing the entire peduncle and flukes. Based on the measurement of the remaining carcass (910 cm.) and similar size female gray whales, we estimated that the intact carcass would have been approximately 12 m. in length. See necropsy report for more details.

locality_detail: Floating 1/2 mile offshore of Daly City
additional_remarks: First reported the morning of April 16th, floating off of Thornton State Beach, Daly City. The carcass reached land at Esplanade Beach that afternoon and was tied off by a TMMC volunteer (37.648621N, 122.496812W). The carcass floated further south to Manor overnight, where it was necropsied the next day. See necropsy report for more details.

locality_detail: Point Reyes National Seashore, Limantour Beach, 1/4 mile west of the trail from the parking lot.
additional_remarks: The animal was first reported on Sunday April 28th floating off shore of Limantour beach. The whale fully came on shore later that evening on the high tide. This carcass was reported still on Limantour Beach on 29 April 2019. We coordinated the necropsy with PRNS and the snowy plover biologist. There were plovers that were nesting in both directions on the beach and we were instructed to not let any pieces float away. Because of this request we could not fully get into the abdominal or the thoracic cavity to assess further. A team from PRNS, TMMC, and CAS performed a necropsy on 30 April 2019. There was one partial and one full-thickness laceration along the caudal peduncle and a partial thickness laceration along the dorsal aspect of the fluke. See necropsy report for more details.

locality_detail: Phillips 66 Refinery, next to the docks
additional_remarks: This animal was initially reported on 5/6/19 floating next to the docks at Phillips 66 Refinery. The animal was tied off to the docks there until boat services were available. On 5/9/19 the animal was then towed to Angel Island. Due to boat availability and tides the team could get out to do the necropsy until 5/14/19. Parts collected for CAS - two pelvic bones, right auditory bullae, small section of baleen, tissue sample.

locality_detail: GGNRA, Ocean Beach at stairway 25.
additional_remarks: Necropsy conducted on 7 May 2019. Right pelvic bone, baleen, barnacles, cyamids, and tissues collected for CAS.

locality_detail: Floating in SF Bay, near Pier 30/32, in between a ship and the dock
additional_remarks: Carcass was towed without permission from NOAA/JV on 05/13 and dropped approximately 8 miles outside the Golden Gate. It washed ashore early 05/14/2019. As of 05/20, unable to tow it back into SF Bay for necropsy.

locality_detail: floating off the western end of Angel Island
additional_remarks: This animal was originally reported by the coast guard in the morning of 5/20/19 floating off the western end of Angel Island in the shipping channel. The coast guard deemed it a shipping hazard and Army Corps of Engineers towed it immediately to Angel Island. The necropsy was carried out on 5/22/19.

locality_detail: Limantour Beach (East Lot), below parking lot on beach, Point Reyes National Seashore
additional_remarks: TMMC media team became aware of carcass, and TMMC responded to get samples and measurements. No necropsy was performed due to time/resource constraints and carcass being in wilderness area.

locality_detail: Point Reyes National Seashore, rolling in the surf at Sculptured Beach
additional_remarks: The carcass was first observed rolling in the surf at Sculputured Beach on 5 July 2019. It was then observed and photographed on the beach near Elephant Rock on 6 July 2019. A team from CAS and TMMC attempted to respond to the carcass on 7 July 2019 but were unable to reach the beach due to the tide. The area where the carcass was located at Elephant Rock was only accessible at a -1.5 tide and all upcoming low tides are too high. Identification of the carcass and all Level A data are from photos.

locality_detail: Point Reyes National Seashore, Wildcat Beach
additional_remarks: This carcass was originally reported to TMMC in the surf at Wildcat Beach in Point Reyes National Seashore on 7/21/2019. PRNS staff confirmed the carcass was still in the same location on 7/22/2019. CAS, TMMC, and PRNS conducted a complete necropsy on 7/23/2019. Although the entire carcass was intact, the skull was fractured with the posterior portion of the cranium loose on the beach. On necropsy the rostrum was fractured and had some bone bruising. COD was blunt force trauma consistent with ship strike.

locality_detail: PRNS - Drake's Beach, east
additional_remarks: Found on BW survey. Data and skeleton collected by Aaron Pahl and Sarah Codde **SWFSC confirmed species and determined gender via DNA

locality_detail: In San Francisco Bay, 1/2 mile NW of Alcatraz Island
additional_remarks: Ship strike witnessed by a member of the public and reported to the USCG, and responded to by the whale watching vessel Kitty Kat. Struck by container ship MSC Azov (captain denies), in SF bay. Whale floated on the surface, bleeding, until it finally appeared to have died. It was last seen floating near Kirby Cove, just west of the Golden Gate Bridge. It did not appear any place on the outer coast after this last sighting. **On October 7th, a report was made to CAS of a "whale sock" (blubber but no bones) at Mussel Rock Park in Daly City. This particular area is known for its high deposition of dead marine mammals. CAS responded to collect tissues for DNA analysis and the blubber was not located. It is unknown if this is the remnant of this humpback, as there were not identifiable features.

locality_detail: Floating between San Francisco and Farallon Islands
additional_remarks: Found as an observation on iNaturalist, posted 06/12/2020. ID'd via photograph

locality_detail: This animal was first reported floating dead 300 yards off the east side of Angel Island floating towards Point Quarry on 3/29 at 0815. The body was later found just east of Horseshoe Bay near the rocky point and was towed over to Angel Island. She was missing her peduncle and fluke from the anus back. A necropsy was performed the next day (3/30).
additional_remarks: Despite COVID-19, a small team was able to get to the carcass and complete a full examination. The whale was missing its peduncle and fluke from the anus back. Based on necropsy examination, we suspect the amputation was caused by killer whale predation, and that this was the cause of death and stranding. The whale was otherwise in good body condition with food in her stomach. Initial samples were collected by TMMC on 3/29 with more complete sampling during necropsy on 3/30. The animal was a decomposition code 2.5 Based on morphological measurements of other stranded gray whales of various ages, the mean ratio of the snout to anus:total length = 0.7+/-0.06 SD. Using this ratio and the snout to anus length of this animal (545 cm), total length was estimated at 779 cm (with +/- 1 SD resulting in a length between 717 cm and 852 cm).

locality_detail: Shelter Cove Point, Monterey Bay National Marine Sanctuary
additional_remarks: Moderately decomposed, skin sloughing off, predated upon by sharks. Due to COVID-19 shelter in place directives, efforts are being focused on fresh dead carcasses, but would otherwise likely have conducted a complete field necropsy.

locality_detail: Canal Boulevard Piers, San Francisco Bay
additional_remarks: Due to COVID-19 pandemic shelter in place directives, efforts are being focused on fresh dead carcasses, but would otherwise likely have conducted a complete field necropsy. Cause of death is unknown.

locality_detail: first observed floating ~ 2 miles off Agate Beach (observed from the end of Poplar Street)
additional_remarks: Full examination not possible due to restrictions in place during the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic. We were unable to bring a large enough team to exam the skeleton for evidence of trauma, the tides were rising, and visitors to the beach were getting too close to the necropsy team. One barnacle collected. skin, blubber, and barnacles were collected.

locality_detail: Chevron Refinery Pier, under long wharf, San Francisco Bay
additional_remarks: COVID-19 impact: (limited sampling and personnel sent due to human health concerns ) cetacean carcass wedged among the pier pilings at the Chevron refinery plant near Richmond Refinery hired Parker Diving to tow the animal out from under the pier and requested by TMMC to land at angel island Barbie Halaska went with Parker Diving during tow operations to collect data

locality_detail: Ohlson Beach
additional_remarks: Response during Covid19 SIP. whale sock void of any bones, other than incomplete set of flipper bones examined by Amy Van Syoc and Trish Miller

locality_detail: Point Reyes Coastline Vista Point, Point Reyes National Seashore
additional_remarks: Carcass was trailing entanglement buoys and line.

locality_detail: Bird Rock, Point Reyes National Seashore
additional_remarks: Carcass found by a shark research group, genetic sample was taken, due to decomposition of the carcass it was not necropsied in the field. Confirmed beaked whale, species identified by SWFSC genetic testing. CAS collected bilateral partial mandibles with two teeth, muscle and skin. Samples sent to SWFSC 6/15/21 in hopes of getting a species identification.

locality_detail: on South East Farallon Island on Mirounga Beach
additional_remarks: Reported by Garrett Duncan of Point Blue Conservation Sciences, who provided a photo to TMMC. Scientist on the island were not able to access the carcass before it washed out with the tide. Small tissue defect over right flank. Unknown if this was received while alive.

locality_detail: North Salmon Creek Beach
additional_remarks: Internal organs retrieved for examination, but carcass was left at site. Cause of death is still open, waiting for histology to confirm. Fetus (73 cm, 8 kg) found in dam.

locality_detail: floating just off off the St Francis Yacht Club/Crissy Field
additional_remarks: Towed to Angel Island by the Army Corps of Engineers on 3/31/21. Appeared fresh on 3/31/21, but was decidedly code 3 on examination 4/1//21 despite both eyes being intact. Histo, frozen tissue, biotox samples, baleen, and both pelvic bones were collected. Cause of death unknown/open.

locality_detail: Floating offshore about 1 mile from Fitzgerald Marine Reserve
other_findings: large gash on maxilla and blood and rotted tissue on the right side of the head, near the back of the skull
additional_remarks: No Covid-19 restrictions of response personnel but Fitzgerald Marine Reserve beach is closed due to staffing issues. Permission obtained. Necropsy completed. right pelvic bone, blubber samples, organ samples, baleen collected for CAS and TMMC archives. Suspected ship strike

locality_detail: Floating in San Francisco Bay south of Angel Island
additional_remarks: Originally reported on 4/5/21 floating south of Angel Island (37.844672, -122.419292). After several failed attempts to locate the carcass on 4/5 and 4/6, it was reported wedged under the Berkeley Marina the afternoon of 4/6. On 4/7, the carcass was towed to Angel Island, and some initial samples were collected (skin, blubber, cyamids). The necropsy was completed on 4/8/2021. Length was borderline for subadult/adult, age determination was made based on immature testes. No signs of trauma were noted and the cause of death is open at this time.

locality_detail: Golden Gate National Recreation Area, Muir Beach
additional_remarks: The carcass was originally reported on 4/8/21 in the surf off of the north end of Muir Beach (37.859631, -122.577060). The carcass was fully ashore during the low tide and a necropsy was completed.

locality_detail: In San Francisco Bay
additional_remarks: dark green line; one bullet buoy, red tip, white butt; buoy appeared to trail whale, buoy went under when whale went under; Presumed Still Entangled; Additional details about this entanglement may be obtained through the NMFS Regional Entanglement Response Coordinator
affiliation: NMFS West Coast Region - Long Beach

locality_detail: Floating off shore west of the Golden Gate Bridge approximately three miles south of the San Francisco Bar buoy
additional_remarks: TMMC first received a report of a dead whale floating off shore west of the Golden Gate Bridge approximately three miles south of the San Francisco Bar buoy the morning of 23 April 2021. The floating carcass was initially reported at 37.704722°N, 122.698333°W. At 1900h later that day (4/23/2021), CAS received a report of a whale carcass washed ashore on Golden Gate National Recreation Area, Fort Funston at 37.714404°N, 122.504758°W. Based on initial photos the whale was identified as a Balaenoptera but species could not be determined using photos due to the position of the carcass on the beach. The morning of 24 April 2021, a small team from CAS confirmed the location of the carcass and a necropsy team was assembled. The necropsy was completed the afternoon of 4/24/2021. Cause of death was determined to be blunt force trauma at necropsy.

locality_detail: Rocks Beach - Keil Cove, San Francisco Bay
additional_remarks: Washed up on private beach, samples collected, cause of death determined as malnutrition.

locality_detail: First reported floating between Alcatraz and Angel Island.
additional_remarks: The RP who called in the whale on May 2nd towed it to Angel Island without permission. We were not permitted to do a full necropsy, but were granted permission to photograph, measure, and cut windows into the sides of the whale to allow it to sink. The whale was extremely bloated with most of the skin on the L side sloughed/predated by birds. Aged as adult based on adult males >11.1m Skin, blubber, muscle, L pelvic bone, and intestinal contents were collected.

locality_detail: San Francisco Bay, Port of Oakland, Berth 20.
additional_remarks: The whale carcass was first reported Monday 5/3/2021 at approximately 1100h at the Port of Oakland, wedged between a ship called the SS Cape Mohican (a large (873-foot) uniquely-shaped military ship designed to carry and load barges) and the pilings at berth 20. The SS Cape Mohican had been in that location for approximately a year. A small team from TMMC and CAS was able to assess the carcass and its location on Wednesday 5/5/2021. For the whale to be removed from the location, the wooden pilings of the berth needed to be cut and the ship needed to be moved. The pilings were cut and the ship was moved on 5/6/2021 by the Port of Oakland. Once the whale was dislodged, it was towed to a nearby floating dock where the small TMMC and CAS team were able to collect minimal samples, photos, and measurements. Because a full necropsy was not preformed, a COD could not be determined.

locality_detail: Rolling in surf at Half Moon Bay State beach (Francis SB)
additional_remarks: UPDATE 5/16/21: whale landed south of original strand area (appx two miles south past Poplar Beach). Was able to get a more accurate length but it is still approximate due to the body being curved. Expelling bones along the two mile stretch of Poplar and Redondo beaches. 5/12/21 No Covid-19 restrictions. Carcass never landed but was rolling in the surf over a few days. A few pieces of skin and one piece of broken skull bone collected for CAS archives. The atlas bone, along with the other cervical verts were seen in the intertidal. Noteworthy is what appears to be bruising inside the atlas bone. I also saw ribs falling out of a hole near the right shoulder.

locality_detail: Floating off of Duxbury Reef
additional_remarks: The carcass was first reported to TMMC floating off of Duxbury Reef in Bolinas (37.8851, -122.6994) late afternoon of 05/12/2021. CAS staff member, Marjorie Cox, confirmed that the carcass was floating off of Duxbury Point. On 5/13/2021 in the morning the carcass was confirmed on land at Agate Beach (37.896599, -122.710106). An initial CAS team took photos, morphometrics, and initial samples on 5/13/2021. With permission the Marin County Parks, the team from TMMC and CAS conducted a complete necropsy at 0730h on 5/14/2021.The carcass was a late code 2, early code 3 at necropsy.

locality_detail: Pacifica State Beach (Linda Mar) where Crespi Dr. intersects with Highway 1
additional_remarks: No Covid-19 restrictions. Carcass was skinless. Collected small amount of marginal skeletal skin and a piece of tongue tissue for DNA. Towed out to sea the day after exam.

locality_detail: Angel Island - Peries Beach
additional_remarks: Measurements and skin sample taken only. No necropsy performed.

locality_detail: Onshore of an inaccessible pocket beach between Tennessee Valley and Muir Beach
additional_remarks: No Covid-19 restrictions. Originally entered as CAS-Er-26May21-SLP due to the fact that nothing was collected and the carcass was not examined due to inaccessibility. On June 22, 2021, TMMC was able to access the carcass after it had floated to Tennessee Valley Beach. TMMC took measurements and tissues for genetics before the carcass washed out again. This whale was initially reported as a beach cast carcass in an inaccessible cove north of Tennessee Valley beach on May 26, 2021. The penis was extruded and skin was missing all over body. On June 5, TMMC received a report of the carcass on the same beach from a hiker at the lookout point at Tennessee Valley Beach. On June 15th, CAS received a report that the carcass was still on the inaccessible beach. See photos below with dates of reports. The length of the carcass was estimated as approximately 32¿ based on the size of the gulls on the carcass in the photo taken on June 5th. The average Western Gull is 2¿ in total length. The carcass was determined to be a unique individual from two other carcasses (CAS-SLP-692/TMMC-C-676 and TMMC-C-674) that stranded around the same date based on photo comparisons of the left flippers, which were visible during each stranding event.

locality_detail: Floating offshore below Point Bonita, Marin Headlands
additional_remarks: No Covid-19 restrictions. Unable to examine. nothing collected. penis extruded ID'd via photo UPDATE 6/3/21: Carcass drifted and landed on Rodeo Beach in the Marin headlands. Full exam done, partial necropsy to examine vertebrae was completed and positive for BFT from the neck area through the upper lumbar region. Pieces of broken vertebrae were collected and archived by CAS, in addition to the left pelvic bone. Tissue samples were collected by both TMMC and CAS

locality_detail: Floating approximately 2 nautical miles offshore from Bolinas
additional_remarks: No Covid restrictions. CAS completed the Level A data and necropsy report based on the series of photos provided by the ACCESS Team and interviews with team members that observed the floating carcass on June 15th. An unidentified whale carcass was first reported floating ~2.0 nautical miles offshore of Bolinas in Marin County by both Tow Boat USA and the US Coast Guard on Friday June 11, 2021. The carcass was reported at 37.866667, -122.716667 (Tow Boat USA) and 37.86772, -122.72197 (US Coast Guard). The initial Tow Boat USA report stated that the carcass was a ¿gray whale calf with prop wounds¿. On Tuesday June 15, CAS received a message from the ACCESS survey team that they located a floating cetacean carcass ~ 6.0 nautical miles off of Bolinas (37.897917, -122.872833). Injuries present in photos from the US Coast Guard and the ACCESS team were used to confirm that the three reports were all the same whale. Using the June 15th photos from ACCESS, based on the flipper size, shape, and coloration, CAS determined that the carcass was a Balaenopteridae, either a fin or sei whale. Photos taken by the ACCESS Team showed several lesions consistent with orca predation events such as: orca rake marks on the flipper, missing mandibles and tongue, abdominal injuries with torn tissue, and peeled skin. No samples were collected since the carcass was floating. The standard length was estimated at 30-35 feet long by the ACCESS Team.

locality_detail: Floating about 5 miles offshore of Pillar Point
additional_remarks: young fin whale in two parts, initially seen floating about 5 miles offshore of Pillar Point. Landed on Mavericks Beach. Initially unidentifiable until I noted the two-toned feature of baleen on the right side. The 1/4 most forward section of baleen was cream-colored, as opposed to the other, which was gray. two sections of baleen collected with about 170 plates

locality_detail: Floating off shore of Ocean Beach at Lincoln.
additional_remarks: The carcass was first reported to TMMC on Sunday afternoon June 20, 2021 offshore at Ocean Beach, San Francisco (37.7640, -122.5201). The carcass did land on shore and was rolling in the surf (37.7665, -122.5129) later that afternoon. CAS received a report that the carcass was on the beach at 0600h on Monday June 21, 2021 (37.769219. -122.512585). The TMMC and CAS team conducted the complete necropsy the afternoon of June 21, 2021. Left pelvic bone, left hyoid bone, muscle samples, 9 baleen plates, cyamids, barnacles collected for CAS archive

locality_detail: Point Reyes National Seashore, just south of McClure's Beach
additional_remarks: On August 2, TMMC received a report of a dead gray whale in Point Reyes National Seashore. A team from TMMC and CAS responded on August 3. The tides and swells prevented a complete necropsy but a partial necropsy was completed on August 3. The carcass was a late code 3, moderate decomposition carcass with extensive autolyzed tissue. As the carcass rolled in the surf the ribs became dislodged and the team found at least three fractured ribs. The team was also able to retrieve the left pelvic bone and it too was fractured, there was no associated hemorrhage or bruising of the bone COD could not be determined due to the state of decomposition and the fact that the carcass had been rolling around in the rocky surf.

locality_detail: Point Reyes National Seashore, South Beach, 1/2 - 3/4 mile south of the parking lot.
additional_remarks: CAS Field responder Augie Niles (NAK), Level A report filed by M. Flannery. On Friday 9/03/21, TMMC received a report of a live whale stranded on South Beach in Point Reyes National Seashore, Marin County (~38.0416, -122.9922). A TMMC veterinary team responded to the whale as soon as possible after the report. The team observed the animal for more than 30 minutes after which time the whale did manage to get itself off the beach/ breakers with some large waves and seemed to be moving off slowly. The whale then stalled with heavy listing and struggling and was observed fully immersed in water and not breathing. It was concluded that the whale had died. Due to the timing late in the day and the fact that the whale was in the water no samples or data were collected during this initial response. On Saturday 9/04/21the carcass was reported on land. Due to the timing of the tides and the necropsy logistics, TMMC, CAS, and PRNS completed the necropsy response on Sunday 9/05/2021.

locality_detail: PRNS - floating in the surf at the north end of North Beach
additional_remarks: No Covid-19 restrictions, pelvic bone and tissues collected for CAS. An unidentified partial whale carcass was first reported floating in the surf at the north end of North Beach, Point Reyes National Seashore (PRNS), Marin County on Wednesday October 27, 2021. The carcass was reported floating at 38.0908, -122.9705 by a PRNS Biologist. At the time of the report, the carcass was continuing to float further north. On Thursday October 28, CAS team member, Marjorie Cox, walked from the North Beach parking lot to the southern edge of Abbott's Lagoon and did not see any signs of the carcass. On Sunday October 31, 2021 a Greater Farallones Association (GFA) Beach Watch Survey Team reported a ~790-cm. humpback whale carcass approximately one mile south of the PRNS North Beach parking lot (38.064444, -122.981667). The advanced decomposition carcass was missing the lower jaws but the rostrum was still present. On Monday November 1, 2021, Marjorie Cox along with PRNS Biologist, Sarah Codde, responded to the location to collect data and samples from the carcass. They estimated the length to be ~810 cm. with the rostrum missing at this point. Several lesions consistent with possible orca predation such as: missing mandibles and tongue, possible orca rake marks on the flukes and flippers, and rounded bite marks and possible rake marks on the abdomen and peduncle.

locality_detail: North end of Del Mar Cove in the Sea Ranch
additional_remarks: No Covid-19 restrictions, small piece of tissue collected for DNA species ID. large piece of abdominal skin which include pleats and an umbilicus, and is void of skin. Floating in and out of this location

locality_detail: Pomponio SB, north of parking lot
additional_remarks: No Covid-19 restrictions, skull, left flipper, multiple diagnostic samples (for TMMC), skin and muscle for CAS archives.

locality_detail: 1 nm west of Mussel Point near Salmon Creek SB
additional_remarks: No Covid-19 restrictions, nothing collected*, ID'd via photographs and video ** *skin, muscle, blubber, earbones X2, baleen section with 21 plates See attached report for details on both encounters **Update: carcass landed on December 6th, and was examined on December 7. Samples and parts collected.

locality_detail: Point Reyes National Seashore, Limantour Beach
additional_remarks: No Covid-19 restrictions. On 3/05/22, TMMC received a report of a live whale stranded at Limantour Beach, Point Reyes National Seashore (~38.0252, -122.8838). The Reporting Party (RP) reported that the animal was seen at 0630-0700 in the morning, was stuck in a shallow pool, and was seen spouting and moving its tail. TMMC responders found the whale stranded slightly offshore at ~1030h. Upon first observation it was unclear to the responders whether or not the animal was still alive. The animal was inaccessible at that time due to the tides. At ~1200h, TMMC responders confirmed that the whale was deceased. TMMC pathology team and PRNS/CAS Marjorie Cox responded in the late afternoon to collect level A. Due to the timing and the tides, the team was only able to take photos, basic measurements, and tissue samples. The team confirmed that the whale was an ~1200cm adult female gray whale. A small team returned to the whale on 3/6/2022 at 0800 during the low tide and were able to collect additional tissue samples (vagina, lung, liver, tongue, intestine, lymph node). On 3/9 a CAS responder revisited the carcass in an effort to collect baleen however the carcass remained in the original position and was partially buried making baleen inaccessible. Blood, histo, infectious disease (n=~15), skin, muscle, blubber, eye, GI content, and aqueous humor samples collected. Blubber sample archived at CAS. Partial rib (cut during initial response) collected on 04/11/2022 for CAS archive.

The entangling gear involved multiple CDFW Commercial Dungeness Crab sets, with at least 4 lines and 4 buoys (2 were reporting party's, 2 were others). The entanglement was wrapped around the whale's left pectoral flipper. Marks on whales back, skin rubbed off by line. Partially Disentangled by Fisherman; Presumed Still Entangled; Additional details about this entanglement may be obtained through the NMFS Regional Entanglement Response Coordinator
affiliation: NMFS West Coast Region - Long Beach

locality_detail: USS Hornet Museum, San Francisco Bay
additional_remarks: Photgraphs and minimal sampling done. Carcass towed to Sausalito shipyard and tied off the Army Corps pier for 2 days. Army Corps towed whale offshore of Brooks Island in Richmond.

locality_detail: Northern end of Muir Beach

locality_detail: North headlands of Pacifica State Beach
other_findings: Trauma to thoracic spine and tissues
additional_remarks: No Covid-19 restrictions, Animal was reported on the evening of Thursday April 28th high on the rocky intertidal area just north of Linda Mar Beach. The animal was bloated but still in good condition. A CAS staff member responded to the original report on April 28 to confirm the carcass condition and access for necropsy. A team from TMMC and CAS conducted the necropsy during the low tide the afternoon of April 29th. Left pelvic bone, barnacles, and Cyamids collected for CAS archives, baleen collected for TMMC and CAS. Trauma to thoracic spine, including edema, hemorrhage and broken vertebra.

locality_detail: Golden Gate National Recreation Area, Fort Funston, ~1/2 mile south of the sand ladder at the pilings
additional_remarks: No Covid restrictions. Left pelvic bone with vestigial femur, 13 baleen plates, and cyamids collected for CAS. Stranding History: On 5/12/2022, TMMC received a report of a large cetacean near shore at Fort Funston (~37.7115, -122.504). The whale was in the surf and the reporting party noted that the fins were moving. A TMMC responder checked on the whale that evening and it appeared to be deceased. On Friday 5/13, the carcass had moved further south and was high and dry on the beach (37.705597, -122.501963). A team from TMMC and CAS responded during the low tide and completed the necropsy. During the initial necropsy, only part of the left side and the ventral side of the carcass was accessible. On Monday 5/16, CAS received a report that the carcass had turned over and the opposite side had become accessible. On 5/17, a small team from CAS revisited the carcass and examined a section of the right lateral and the dorsal side of the carcass. Upon examination on 5/17, there was edema and hemorrhage in the subcutis posterior to the eye into the cervical region and skeletal muscle hemorrhage into the thoracic area (macerated epaxial muscle) and T2-T9 each had short complete fractures on the dorsal spinous processes.

locality_detail: On Miramontes Point/Manhattan beach
additional_remarks: No Covid-19 restrictions, both pelvic bones and atlas/c2/c3 collected for CAS, baleen collected for research and education. There was a regionally extensive area of subcuticular and muscular hemorrhage and edema extending from the base of the neck on the right side to the caudal aspect of the thorax (approx. 400 cm cranial to caudal) and from the sternum to the limit of dorsal dissection (carcass in dorsal recumbency). The skeletal muscle was markedly macerated, pale brown and generally liquified. The ribs of the right thoracic wall were displaced in a medio-caudal direction but no fractures were detected. The left occipital condyle was displaced 43cm from the articular facet on C1 (atlas bone). There was an approximately 12cm diameter by 2cm deep defect on the lateral aspect of the C1 lateral articular facet and coarse irregular margins on the lateral aspect of the left occipital condyle (avulsion fractures). The right occipital condyle and C1 articular facet were displaced to a lesser extent (approximately 10cm). **See necropsy report for more information**

locality_detail: San Gregorio SB
additional_remarks: No Covid-19 restrictions, both earbones collected for CAS archives, no gray whales of this size reported in the last couple of years in this area. Very strong northern swell may have pushed this ashore, Cranium only present. Not measured but small enough for me to manipulate.

locality_detail: Pomponio SB
additional_remarks: No Covid-19 restrictions, one flipper bone and tissue sample collected for DNA analysis, largely unidentifiable except for tail flukes, which measured about 200 cms from notch to end of the lobe on one side. in and out of surf so difficult to measure. One expelled vertebra was snapped off at the dorsal process Confirmed by SWFSC to be a Physeter macrocephalus on 9/2/25 by Brittany Hancock-Hanser

locality_detail: On Bolinas Beach, north of Brighton Road entrance
additional_remarks: No Covid-19 restrictions, left pelvic bone, a section of baleen, and one barnacle collected for CAS archives. Preliminary exam by TMMC on 3/23. Full necropsy performed on 03/25/2023 by TMMC and CAS. We found an area of blunt force trauma on the left lateral head extending from the eye to the mid scapula moving up towards the dorsal aspect. There was dark red staining (hemorrhage) within in the blubber, subcutaneous fat, and skeletal muscle in that same location. The muscle surrounding the base of the skull and C1 was macerated and easily pulled apart. We examined the base of the skull, all the left lateral aspects of vertebra for any fractures but could not find any. We would not examine the entire head due to the recumbency and nor the extent of the area with hemorrhage. The muscle surrounding the other vertebrae was of normal consistency and color. No other significant findings in this animal apart from a good blubber layer and subcutaneous fat.

locality_detail: Floating offshore of the bay trail, south of San Leandro Marina
additional_remarks: No Covid-19 restrictions, Team from TMMC and CAS performed a necropsy, finding evidence of ship strike. See Necropsy report for details and photos, bilateral pelvic bones, right ear bone and 205 plates of baleen collected for CAS archives

locality_detail: just south of Angel Island State Park in San Francisco Bay
additional_remarks: Collision wit Sailboat - 38ft in length with a Yanmar Fuse 30A brand new engine and 29 horsepower with a 16" prop diameter (3 prop blades) The prop is positioned between the keel (4'6") and the rudder. Collision Information: The vessel was sailing at 3.5-4 knots at the time of impact and the engine was on idle, they were sailing NE and were trying to tack to S-SE at the time of the collision. Water depth at the time of the collision was 38'. The whale came up from underneath them, they heard a very large noise upon impact (the captain thought they were running aground it was so loud) and then they (4 people total on board in the cockpit) saw the back of the animal and a mud plume with algae and seaweed around the animal. They did not see the whale surface again or any blood in the water.
affiliation: NMFS West Coast Region - Long Beach

locality_detail: Bodega Head, Bodega Bay

On 21 June 2023 a CAS staff member responded, but was unable to access the whale due to tide level. On 24 June 2023 a small team from CAS was able to access the whale for a limited external exam. Due to the location and tides, there was no necropsy performed. The whale was in a state of advanced decomposition and in dorsal recumbency, making nutritional condition difficult to determine. Approximately 75% of skin was sloughed off from the ventral surface.

locality_detail: Salmon Creek Beach
additional_remarks: USCG self-report; The sea state was approximately 1-2 foot swells, winds were less than 05 knots, and visibility was unrestricted near Salmon Creek Beach where the incident occurred. Lookouts were stationed as vessel was traveling at a cruising speed of ~20 knots and 1850 RPM’s at the time of the whale strike. Prior to the strike, several crewmembers observed whale activity approximately 01-02 nautical miles off of the bow of the MLB. Multiple course changes of 15-20 degrees to starboard were made to give the sighted whales a wide berth. No additional whales were reported in the immediate vicinity of the MLB just prior to the strike. At 0934 PST, the crew of the MLB reported feeling the boat shudder as it hit something in the water. Immediately after feeling the strike, the crew looked behind the MLB and observed a whale of unknown species surface, roll, and dive. No visible injury to the whale or blood in the water was reported by any of the crew members interviewed. An underway performance check was completed and vibrations were noticed above 1300 RPM. Upon haulout, damage sustained was a bent propellor on the port side of the vessel which is believed to have been caused by striking the whale.
affiliation: NMFS West Coast Region - Long Beach

locality_detail: Half Moon Bay
additional_remarks: USCG self-report; Sea state was 1-2 foot swells, winds 10 kts and visibility unrestricted. Transiting at cruising speed of ~18kts and 1850 RPMs at the time of the strike. Came to all stop to investigate and immediately saw brown water (blood) patch roughly 5'x5' with 1'x1' marine ligament floating (photographs of the debris were taken and included with final LIR). Shaft leak on #2 MDE shaft seal; roughly 30 drops per minute. Engineering rectified by securing the shaft. Steering test completed as well as vibration/propulsion checks. At the time of strike, all members of the bridge team were actively lookout out. No marine animals nor activity sighed prior to, or following, the strike.

Entire cranium missing and bone fragments found in tissues, part of tongue still present and rostral bones are completely present, mandibles were jutting forward of the maxilla, no cervical verts were identified and spine was disarticulated in the lower thorax

other_findings: prop strike to right lateral side of carcass
additional_remarks: floating free in San Francisco Bay after being stuck under a Phillips 66 dock at Point Davis for four days. Whale was being confined under the dock by two wooden boards. Once the were cut out to tie the whale to a tow vessel, the whale floated free into the bay and was left to float by Phillips 66. It was reported by Jim Ferris of Phillips 66 that the whales head was protruding from under the boards many times, so he was not surprised that the whale moved with the ebbing tide when they removed them. COD: sharp force trauma (5 propeller cuts through right lateral side)

locality_detail: About 1 mile due south of Angel Island, about half way between Angel and Alcatraz
additional_remarks: Reporting Party Details: We were headed east (downwind), full sails, with probably 10 knots of wind, and 5 knots of boat speed. The whale surfaced a few meters to our left moving mostly in our direction. Couldn't tell it's full size, but the visible portion was maybe a meter wide and a few meters long above water. It then seemed to turn south towards us - we hadn't changed course, and we hit its midsection straight on. We were jostled, but not hurt, and no visible damage to the boat (keel is yet to be assessed). We couldn't see the whale immediately after the collision, but did see a whale about half mile away a few minutes later closer to Angel Island (south side). I called in the incident to the coast guard. We don't have any photos. There were 2 other sailors on board at the time. Let me know if you need more info.
affiliation: NMFS West Coast Region - Long Beach

locality_detail: Point Reyes National Seashore on beach below Commonweal
additional_remarks: Initial response was data and small samples only, full necropsy conducted on 5/23/2025. Numerous depressions consistent with shark bite marks. Focal full thickness blubber congestion at intrascapular region. The focal congestion of the intrascapular blubber was associated with hemorrhage, distinguished as marked darkening of the musculature and the presence of black/red, gelatinous deposits at the dorsum, extending over the atlantooccipital joint and ventrally, under both scapulae, and around the external aspect of the left scapula. Incision of the musculature at this site released large volumes of red fluid, which were not released upon incision of muscle at other locations. Trauma likely due to vessel strike.
